title: Slow-cooker pulled pork
subtitle: A whole shoulder, dry-rubbed, eight hours on low.
excerpt: A 2.5 kg pork shoulder dry-rubbed with smoked paprika, brown sugar, and salt; cooked low and slow in the slow cooker for eight to ten hours; shredded with two forks; dressed with a cider-vinegar sauce. Twenty-five minutes of work; dinner for eight; freezer portions for next week.
sourceNotes:
The technical core of slow-cooked pork shoulder (long low heat to break down collagen, internal temperature around 91°C for shreddable texture) draws on public-domain US Department of Agriculture extension material on pork cookery.

The broader American Southern barbecue tradition has thin public-domain literature pre-1928; the recipe descended through West African enslaved-cook traditions adapted by white slaveholders, formalised through twentieth-century Southern cookbooks that are not yet in public domain. The dish here is the slow-cooker adaptation, calibrated for a 6-litre domestic slow cooker, with the Carolina-style cider-vinegar sauce that pairs best with a dry-rubbed shoulder.

recipe.yieldDescription: about 1.5 kg shredded meat; fills 8 brioche buns generously, with portions left over
recipe.freezeNotes: Shredded pork freezes well for three months in airtight tubs with a ladle of cooking juices over each portion. Defrost overnight in the fridge; reheat gently in a covered pan on the hob or in a 160°C oven for fifteen minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles into a 6.5-litre slow cooker if the shoulder fits without touching the lid. For a larger party, two slow cookers run side by side; one shoulder doesn't really make a small batch.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dry rub can be made up and stored in a jar for a month. The pork can be rubbed the night before and rested in the fridge so the salt has time to season deep into the meat. The whole dish is itself a make-ahead recipe; pulled pork on day two is at least as good as on day one, and the cooking juices set into a savoury jelly in the fridge.
recipe.temperatureNote: slow cooker on the LOW setting; ~85°C inside the pot

recipeTools.notes: 6-litre size for a 2.5 kg shoulder. Smaller cookers need a smaller cut; the pork must fit with the lid sealing properly.
For straining the cooking juices into the sauce.
Optional — for searing the shoulder before it goes into the slow cooker, if you want more colour in the finished meat.

glossaryTerms.definition: The slow conversion of tough connective-tissue collagen into soft gelatine that happens when meat sits at around 75–95°C for a long time. The temperature window matters: hotter and the muscle fibres dry out before the collagen breaks down; cooler and the conversion never starts. Cuts heavy in collagen (shoulder, shin, oxtail, cheek) reward this kind of cooking; cuts low in it (loin, fillet) don't.

The cut taken from the upper front leg of the pig. The American name is Boston butt, even though it comes from the front rather than the back. The cut is heavy in connective tissue and intramuscular fat, both of which make it ideal for long slow cooking. Look for a piece with the fat cap still on; the fat melts into the meat over the long cook and is what gives the pulled meat its glossy weight.

content.text: A 2.5 kg
pork shoulder

rubbed with smoked paprika, brown sugar, and salt; cooked in the slow cooker on the low setting for eight to ten hours; pulled apart with two forks; dressed with a Carolina-style cider-vinegar sauce. Twenty-five minutes of active work; dinner for eight in brioche buns; freezer portions for next week.

The shoulder is the right cut for the job. It carries heavy bands of intramuscular fat and a thick cap of connective tissue, both of which break down over the long cook into gelatine that holds the shredded meat together. Pork loin, by comparison, has neither, and goes from raw to overcooked in about forty minutes; it has no place in this recipe.
The slow cooker on the low setting sits at around 85°C, which is the right temperature for
collagen conversion

without drying the muscle. The dry rub seasons the surface and draws a little moisture out, which mixes with the rendered fat to form the dark cooking juices in the bottom of the pot. The Carolina-style finish is cider vinegar plus a few aromatics: sharp, light, and made to cut the richness of the meat rather than pile sweetness on top of it.

attrs.title: Why low, not high
attrs.body: Most slow cookers offer a high setting that runs around 95°C and finishes a shoulder in five hours. It works, but the meat shreds drier and the cooking juices have less depth. The low setting at around 85°C earns its eight to ten hours. If you're at home in the morning to start it, low is the right call. If you're starting in the late afternoon and want dinner by eight, high gets the job done.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: bone-in if you can get it; fat cap left on
sliced thick, for the bottom of the pot
optional, for a slightly sweeter sauce

content.text: A 6-litre slow cooker is the right size for a 2.5 kg shoulder; smaller cookers need a smaller cut. The other kit is everyday: tongs, a medium bowl for the rub, a small saucepan for the sauce, a fine-mesh sieve to strain the cooking juices. A large heavy frying pan for searing is optional but worth the dish-wash if you want more colour in the finished meat.
Method
Mix the rub

In a medium bowl, whisk together {{light-brown-sugar}} of light brown sugar, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine salt, {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, {{english-mustard-powder}} of English mustard powder, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{garlic}} of granulated garlic, and {{cayenne}} of cayenne. The rub should be brick-red and clumpy from the sugar; break up any large clumps with the back of a spoon.
Rub the shoulder

Pat the pork shoulder dry with kitchen paper. Set it on a chopping board. Sprinkle a quarter of the rub onto the meat and massage it into every face, including under the skin where the fat cap meets the meat. Repeat with the remaining rub until the shoulder is coated all over in a thick red crust. If you have time, wrap loosely in cling film and rest in the fridge overnight; if not, rest at room temperature for fifteen minutes while the cooker warms up.
Sear the shoulder (optional but worth it)

Heat a heavy 30 cm frying pan over a high heat without oil; the rub has enough sugar to caramelise on its own and the fat cap renders fast. Sear the shoulder on each side for two minutes (five sides on a typical squared-off cut) until each face is a deep mahogany colour. This step is optional but adds visible colour and a richer base note to the cooking juices. Skip if pushed for time.
Build the pot

Lay slices of {{onion}} across the base of the slow-cooker insert; they lift the shoulder off the bottom and stop it scorching. Pour in {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar. Set the shoulder on top of the onions, fat cap up. Don't add water; the shoulder releases plenty of moisture over the cook.
Cook low and slow

Cover with the lid and cook on the low setting for eight to ten hours. The shoulder is done when a fork pushed into the thickest part meets no resistance and twists freely; the meat should pull away from the bone with no effort. If the fork meets resistance, give it another hour and check again. The internal temperature at this point is around 91°C; some thermometers will read higher in spots.
Rest and pull

Lift the shoulder out of the cooker with tongs onto a large board and rest for thirty minutes. The meat keeps cooking gently as it rests and the muscle fibres relax. Pour the cooking juices through a fine-mesh sieve into a jug, pressing the onions to extract every bit of liquid; discard the spent onions. Skim off the layer of fat that rises to the top of the juices.

Pick the bone out of the shoulder; it should slip free with a small twist. Discard the bone. Pull the meat apart into long shreds with two forks, working with the grain. Tip the shredded meat into a large bowl and pour over a few ladles of the skimmed cooking juices to keep it moist.
Make the cider-vinegar sauce

Pour {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar into a small saucepan with {{light-brown-sugar}} of brown sugar, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, {{english-mustard}} of English mustard, and {{ketchup}} of ketchup if using. Bring to a simmer over a medium heat and cook for three minutes until the sugar dissolves and the sauce thickens lightly. Taste and adjust with fine salt; the sauce should be sharp and slightly hot, not sweet. Pour half the sauce over the shredded pork and toss; serve the rest at the table.
Serve

Pile the shredded pork into toasted brioche buns, top with a generous spoonful of coleslaw, and extra sauce on the side. Coleslaw is non-negotiable: the cold crunch and the vinegar in the dressing are what make the sandwich work. Sweet potato fries or a green salad round the plate out.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Pulled pork is forgiving in the long cook but trips at the edges: under-cooked at one end, dry at the other, or finished with the wrong texture.

items.symptom: Meat still tough and won't shred after eight hours.
items.fix: Give it another one to two hours on low. The fork test is the reliable one: if it goes in and twists freely, the meat is done. Some shoulders take longer than others; bone-in pieces in particular need the full ten hours.

items.symptom: Meat shredded but dry and stringy.
items.cause: Cooked too long, or pulled without saving the juices.
items.fix: Always strain the cooking juices and toss the shredded pork in a few ladles before serving. The juices carry both moisture and flavour; pulled pork without them is just dry meat. If the cooker ran past the eight-to-ten window, more juices fix most of the damage.

items.symptom: Cooking juices very greasy and pale.
items.cause: Shoulder had too much fat cap, or wasn't seared first.
items.fix: Skim the fat off the strained juices before they go back into the pulled meat; a layer floats to the top within a few minutes. A pre-sear caramelises the surface and gives the juices a darker, deeper base note.

items.symptom: Sauce dominates the meat; pork tastes only of vinegar.
items.fix: Dress lightly: half the sauce on the meat, the other half at the table. Pulled pork should taste of pork first and sauce second. If the bowl is over-sauced, add a ladle of plain reserved juices to dilute.

content.text: Variations

A sweeter Kansas City-style sauce uses molasses, brown sugar, tomato, and Worcestershire in place of the sharp Carolina sauce; pour over the meat and reduce by a third. A Memphis-style version skips the sauce on the meat and lets the rub do all the work, serving the sauce only at the table. The shoulder can also be cooked in a low oven at 130°C conventional, tightly wrapped in foil over a roasting tin, for the same eight to ten hours; the slow cooker is the lazier route.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

The whole recipe is a make-ahead. Day-two pulled pork is at least as good as day-one; the cooking juices set into a savoury jelly in the fridge that re-melts on reheating. Shredded portions freeze well for three months in airtight tubs with a ladle of juices spooned over the top. Defrost overnight in the fridge; reheat gently in a covered pan on the hob with a splash of water, or in a 160°C oven for fifteen minutes.

Leftover pulled pork is the start of half a dozen other dinners: tossed through pasta with a quick tomato sauce, piled into a quesadilla with cheese and pickled jalapeño, layered into a baked potato with sour cream and chives, or stirred into a quick noodle soup with stock and spring onion.
Where this dish lives

Pulled pork is American Southern barbecue, with roots running deeper than the cookbooks. The technique of long low cooking over wood smoke came into the Carolinas through West African enslaved cooks who adapted their own pit-cooking traditions to local pork; the white slaveholders took the technique and the credit, and it survived through the twentieth century as the dominant Southern barbecue tradition. Different states, different sauces: Carolina cider-vinegar (sharp, thin), Memphis dry rub, Kansas City molasses-tomato (thick, sweet), Texas beef brisket (a parallel meat tradition).

The slow-cooker version is a domestic compromise; a Crock-Pot at low for ten hours can't match a true wood-smoke pit, but it gets the meat to the same shreddable texture, and most home cooks don't have a smoker in the garden. Slow-cooker pulled pork landed in British home kitchens in the 2010s via the American food media; it became one of the standout dishes the appliance category justified, since it is exactly the kind of slow low cooking the slow cooker was designed for.

attrs.quote: Barbecue is the closest thing this country has to a peasant cuisine.
attrs.attribution: John Egerton, Southern Food (1987) — paraphrased; not citable as public domain

content.text: On a Sunday with eight people coming over and time to start the cooker at breakfast, this is the dish. Twenty-five minutes of work in the morning, the smell of slow-cooked pork through the house all afternoon, and dinner ready when the doorbell rings. The freezer portions are the second prize; pulled-pork sandwiches on a Tuesday from the bottom of the freezer are one of the better wins a meal-prep cook gets.
